| E459 | Judah sentenced Tamar: Bring her forth, and let her be burnt -- pre-Sinai capital treatment of sexual immorality | Gen 38:24 | Neutral | cmd-08 |  |
| E460 | God created humanity male and female and commanded Be fruitful and multiply -- the male-female distinction and reproductive union established at creation | Gen 1:27-28 | Neutral | cmd-08 |  |
| E461 | Thou shalt not steal -- the eighth commandment. Hebrew: lo tignob. Ganab (H1589) = to take by stealth. Qal Imperfect 2ms, apodictic prohibition. | Exo 20:15 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E462 | Neither shalt thou steal -- Deuteronomic restatement of the eighth commandment. | Deu 5:19 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E463 | He that stealeth a man, and selleth him, or if he be found in his hand, he shall surely be put to death -- man-stealing (kidnapping) is a capital offense. The verb is ganab. | Exo 21:16 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E464 | If a man be found stealing any of his brethren of the children of Israel, and maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him; then that thief shall die; and thou shalt put evil away from among you | Deu 24:7 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E465 | If a man shall steal an ox, or a sheep, and kill it, or sell it; he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep -- graded restitution for property theft. | Exo 22:1 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E466 | If the theft be certainly found in his hand alive...he shall restore double -- 2x restitution for stolen property recovered alive. | Exo 22:4 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E467 | If a thief be found breaking up, and be smitten that he die, there shall no blood be shed for him -- no bloodguilt for killing a nighttime thief. If the sun be risen upon him, there shall be blood shed for him. | Exo 22:2-3 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E468 | If a soul sin, and commit a trespass against the LORD, and lie unto his neighbour in that which was delivered him to keep...he shall restore it in the principal, and shall add the fifth part more thereto -- fraud against a neighbor is a trespass against the LORD; restitution is principal plus 20%. | Lev 6:2-5 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E469 | They shall confess their sin...and he shall recompense his trespass with the principal thereof, and add unto it the fifth part thereof -- confession and restitution (principal + 20%). | Num 5:7 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E470 | Ye shall not steal, neither deal falsely, neither lie one to another -- three prohibitions linked: ganab (steal), kachash (deal falsely), shaqar (lie). Communal plural (2mp). | Lev 19:11 | Neutral | cmd-09 | cmd-10 |
| E471 | Thou shalt not defraud (ashaq, H6231) thy neighbour, neither rob (gazal, H1497) him: the wages of him that is hired shall not abide with thee all night until the morning | Lev 19:13 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |

| E481 | Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse, that there may be meat in mine house, and prove me now herewith, saith the LORD of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven, and pour you out a blessing | Mal 3:10 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E482 | He is a merchant, the balances of deceit are in his hand: he loveth to oppress (ashaq). And Ephraim said, Yet I am become rich...in all my labours they shall find none iniquity in me that were sin | Hos 12:7-8 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E483 | When will the new moon be gone, that we may sell corn? and the sabbath, that we may set forth wheat, making the ephah small, and the shekel great, and falsifying the balances by deceit? | E484 | Are there yet the treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked, and the scant measure that is abominable? Shall I count them pure with the wicked balances, and with the bag of deceitful weights? | Mic 6:10-11 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |

| E496 | Thou shalt do no murder, Thou shalt not commit adultery, Thou shalt not steal, Thou shalt not bear false witness -- Jesus quotes the eighth commandment to the rich young ruler as part of the commandments for eternal life. | Mat 19:18 | Neutral | cmd-09 | cmd-10 |
| E497 | Do not commit adultery, Do not kill, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Defraud not (apostereo, G650), Honour thy father and mother -- Mark's account uniquely adds Defraud not to Jesus's commandment list. | Mrk 10:19 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |

| E499 | He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up some other way, the same is a thief and a robber...The thief cometh not, but for to steal, and to kill, and to destroy: I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly | Jhn 10:1,10 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E500 | Zacchaeus stood, and said unto the Lord; Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor; and if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold. And Jesus said unto him, This day is salvation come to this house | Luk 19:8-9 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |

| E503 | Let him that stole steal no more: but rather let him labour, working with his hands the thing which is good, that he may have to give to him that needeth -- three-stage transformation: (1) stop stealing, (2) labor, (3) give to those in need. | Eph 4:28 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |
| E504 | Nor thieves (kleptai), nor covetous (pleonektai)...nor extortioners (harpages), shall inherit the kingdom of God. And such were some of you: but ye are washed, but ye are sanctified, but ye are justified | 1 Cor 6:10-11 | Neutral | cmd-09 | cmd-11 |

| E507 | That no man go beyond and defraud his brother in any matter: because that the Lord is the avenger of all such | 1 Th 4:6 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |

| E510 | Let none of you suffer as a murderer, or as a thief, or as an evildoer, or as a busybody in other men's matters | 1 Pet 4:15 | Neutral | cmd-09 |  |

| E528 | One witness cannot produce a death sentence: one witness shall not testify against any person to cause him to die | Num 35:30 | Neutral | cmd-10 |  |

| E535 | Lying lips are abomination to the LORD: but they that deal truly are his delight | Pro 12:22 | Neutral | cmd-10 |  |

| E537 | A true witness delivereth souls: but a deceitful witness speaketh lies | Pro 14:25 | Neutral | cmd-10 |  |

| E570 | Thou shalt not covet (chamad, H2530) thy neighbours house, thou shalt not covet thy neighbours wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, nor his ox, nor his ass, nor any thing that is thy neighbours. | Exo 20:17 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E571 | Neither shalt thou desire (chamad, H2530) thy neighbours wife, neither shalt thou covet (hitavveh, Hithpael of avah, H183) thy neighbours house, his field, or his manservant, or his maidservant, his ox, or his ass, or any thing that is thy neighbours. Deuteronomy uses two different verbs and reverses the order. | Deu 5:21 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E572 | When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired (nechmad, Niphal of chamad, H2530) to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof and did eat. The first sin uses the same verb root as the tenth commandment. | Gen 3:6 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E573 | When I saw among the spoils a goodly Babylonish garment, and two hundred shekels of silver, and a wedge of gold of fifty shekels weight, then I coveted (chamad, H2530) them, and took them. Achan confesses to coveting. | Josh 7:21 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E574 | David saw Bathsheba, the wife of Uriah the Hittite, sent messengers, and took her. Davids coveting of his neighbours wife led to adultery. | 2 Sam 11:2-4 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E575 | David wrote to Joab: Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire ye from him, that he may be smitten, and die. Davids coveting led to the murder of Uriah. | 2 Sam 11:15 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E576 | Ahab coveted Naboths vineyard; Naboth refused on the basis of ancestral inheritance. Ahab laid him down upon his bed, and turned away his face, and would eat no bread. | 1 Ki 21:2-4 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E577 | God said to Ahab: Hast thou killed, and also taken possession? God explicitly links Ahabs coveting to murder and theft. | 1 Ki 21:19 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E578 | Gehazi said: I will run after him, and take somewhat of him. He lied to Naaman (My master hath sent me) and was struck with leprosy for covetousness. | 2 Ki 5:20,22,27 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E579 | They covet (chamad, H2530) fields, and take them by violence; and houses, and take them away: so they oppress a man and his house, even a man and his heritage. | Mic 2:2 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E580 | For the wicked boasteth of his hearts desire, and blesseth the covetous, whom the LORD abhorreth. | Psa 10:3 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E581 | Incline my heart unto thy testimonies, and not to covetousness. | Psa 119:36 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E582 | He that loveth silver shall not be satisfied with silver; nor he that loveth abundance with increase: this is also vanity. | Ecc 5:10 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E583 | I had not known sin, but by the law: for I had not known lust (epithumian), except the law had said, Thou shalt not covet (Ouk epithumeseis). Paul specifically quotes the tenth commandment as the law that exposed his sin nature. | Rom 7:7 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E584 | Sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence (epithumian). For without the law sin was dead. Sin used the tenth commandment as a beachhead (aphorme) to produce desire. | Rom 7:8 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E585 | Every man is tempted, when he is drawn away of his own lust (epithumias), and enticed. Then when lust (epithumia) hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death. The source of temptation is internal desire. | Jas 1:14-15 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E586 | For all that is in the world, the lust of the flesh, and the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life, is not of the Father, but is of the world. Three categories of worldly desire. | 1 Jn 2:16 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E587 | From whence come wars and fightings among you? come they not hence, even of your lusts (epithumion) that war in your members? Ye lust (epithumeite), and have not: ye kill, and desire to have, and cannot obtain. Coveting produces murder and conflict. | Jas 4:1-2 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E588 | Take heed, and beware of covetousness (pleonexias): for a mans life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth. Jesus direct warning against covetousness. | Luk 12:15 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E589 | The rich fool said: Soul, thou hast much goods laid up for many years; take thine ease, eat, drink, and be merry. God said: Thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee. So is he that layeth up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God. | Luk 12:19-21 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E590 | From within, out of the heart of men, proceed evil thoughts, adulteries, fornications, murders, thefts, covetousness (pleonexiai)... All these evil things come from within, and defile the man. Jesus locates covetousness as a heart-sin. | Mrk 7:21-23 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E591 | Where your treasure is, there will your heart be also. The heart follows the treasure. | Mat 6:21 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E592 | No man can serve two masters... Ye cannot serve God and mammon. | Mat 6:24 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E593 | But godliness with contentment (autarkeia) is great gain. For we brought nothing into this world, and it is certain we can carry nothing out. And having food and raiment let us be therewith content. | 1 Tim 6:6-8 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E594 | They that will be rich fall into temptation and a snare, and into many foolish and hurtful lusts (epithumias), which drown men in destruction and perdition. | 1 Tim 6:9 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E595 | The love of money (philarguria) is the root of all evil: which while some coveted after (oregomenoi), they have erred from the faith, and pierced themselves through with many sorrows. | 1 Tim 6:10 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E596 | Let your conversation be without covetousness (aphilarguros); and be content with such things as ye have: for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee. The ground for contentment is Gods promise of presence. | Heb 13:5 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E597 | I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content (autarkes). I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me. Pauls contentment is learned and enabled by Christ. | Php 4:11,13 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E598 | Now these things were our examples, to the intent we should not lust after evil things (epithumetas kakon), as they also lusted. Neither be ye idolaters. Israels wilderness lusting is a warning. | 1 Cor 10:6-7 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E599 | Through covetousness (pleonexia) shall they with feigned words make merchandise of you. An heart they have exercised with covetous practices. Following the way of Balaam, who loved the wages of unrighteousness. | 2 Pe 2:3,14-15 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E600 | Put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make not provision for the flesh, to fulfil the lusts (epithumias) thereof. The antidote to fleshly desire is putting on Christ. | Rom 13:14 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E601 | Walk in the Spirit, and ye shall not fulfil the lust (epithumian) of the flesh. For the flesh lusteth (epithumei) against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh. | Gal 5:16-17 | Neutral | cmd-11 | cmd-13, cmd-14 |
| E602 | They that are Christs have crucified the flesh with the affections and lusts (epithumiais). | Gal 5:24 | Neutral | cmd-11 | cmd-14 |
| E603 | O that there were such an heart in them, that they would fear me, and keep all my commandments always, that it might be well with them, and with their children for ever! God desires a heart-orientation toward obedience. | Deu 5:29 | Neutral | cmd-11 | cmd-13 |
| E604 | Lust not after her beauty in thine heart. OT anticipation of the internal prohibition. | Pro 6:25 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E605 | So are the ways of every one that is greedy of gain; which taketh away the life of the owners thereof. Greed is self-destructive. | Pro 1:19 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E606 | He coveteth greedily all the day long: but the righteous giveth and spareth not. Coveting contrasted with righteous generosity. | Pro 21:26 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E607 | The world passeth away, and the lust thereof: but he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever. What is coveted is temporal; obedience is eternal. | 1 Jn 2:17 | Neutral | cmd-11 |  |
| E609 | Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself: I am the LORD. | Lev 19:18 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E610 | Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy. The love command is embedded in the holiness code. | Lev 19:2 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E611 | What doth the LORD thy God require of thee, but to fear the LORD thy God, to walk in all his ways, and to love him, and to serve the LORD thy God with all thy heart and with all thy soul, To keep the commandments of the LORD. | Deu 10:12-13 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E612 | The LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart...to love the LORD thy God with all thine heart. The ability to love God is a divine gift. | Deu 30:6 | Neutral | cmd-12 | cmd-13 |

| E614 | O how love I thy law! it is my meditation all the day. | Psa 119:97 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E615 | The first angel's message in Rev 14:7 uses creation language verbally paralleling the fourth commandment's rationale: 'worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea' (Rev 14:7) echoes 'in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is' (Exo 20:11). | Rev 14:7; Exo 20:11 | Neutral | cmd-16 |  |
| E621 | Love your enemies, bless them that curse you. Jesus extends love beyond the neighbor to the enemy. | Mat 5:44 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E622 | Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect. Love is measured by God's character. | Mat 5:48 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E623 | All things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets. | Mat 7:12 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E624 | A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you. The new standard of love is Christ's self-sacrifice. | Jhn 13:34 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E625 | If ye love me, keep my commandments. Love is the motive; obedience is the expression. | Jhn 14:15 | Neutral | cmd-12 | cmd-13, cmd-14, cmd-15 |

| E630 | He that loveth another hath fulfilled the law. | Rom 13:8 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |
| E631 | The love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us. Love is Spirit-produced. | Rom 5:5 | Neutral | cmd-12 | cmd-13, cmd-14 |

| E637 | Hereby we do know that we know him, if we keep his commandments. Knowing God is tested by obedience. | 1 Jhn 2:3 | Neutral | cmd-12 | cmd-13, cmd-15 |

| E642 | God is love. Love is an attribute of God's nature. | 1 Jhn 4:8,16 | Neutral | cmd-12 |  |

## NECESSARY IMPLICATIONS (N)

| ID | Necessary Implication | Based On | Why Unavoidable | Classification | First Appeared | Also In |
|----|----------------------|-|-|-|-|-|
| N001 | The Bible presents two different modes of delivery: God spoke the Decalogue directly to the people; subsequent laws were given through Moses as mediator |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N002 | The Bible assigns two different repositories: the Decalogue inside the ark, the book of the law beside the ark |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N003 | The Bible attributes two different authorships: God wrote the Decalogue on stone, Moses wrote the book of the law in a book |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N004 | Moses draws an explicit two-part distinction in Deu 4:13-14: God declared/wrote the covenant (ten commandments) vs. God commanded Moses to teach statutes and judgments |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N005 | He added no more marks a boundary after which God ceased speaking directly to the assembly; all subsequent legislation was mediated through Moses |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N006 | The replacement tablets contained the same content as the originals -- both Exo 34:1 and Deu 10:4 state the words that were in the first tables / according to the first writing |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N007 | Paul identifies the specific law he calls holy, just, good, and spiritual as the Decalogue by quoting the tenth commandment (Thou shalt not covet) in Rom 7:7 |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 | cmd-11 |
| N008 | James identifies the royal law and law of liberty as the Decalogue by quoting the sixth and seventh commandments in Jas 2:11 |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N009 | The Decalogue existed on two physical tablets (two tables of stone), creating a structural basis for a two-part division |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N010 | The Decalogue is equated with his covenant (berith) in Deu 4:13 and called words of the covenant in Exo 34:28, making the Decalogue the terms of God's covenant |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| N011 | The new covenant writes my law on hearts (Jer 31:33; Heb 8:10) -- the change is in mode (stone to heart) since the content (my law) remains the same |  |  | Neutral | cmd-01 | cmd-13, cmd-14 |
| N012 | The first commandment prohibits all worship of any being other than YHWH -- the word no (lo) combined with the universal scope of before my face (al panay) allows no exceptions. | E068, E069, E070 | The Hebrew construction lo yihyeh-lekha is a complete negation; al panay (in my presence) is universal since God is everywhere; therefore no other god is permitted under any circumstance. | Neutral | cmd-02 |  |
| N013 | Gods jealousy (qanna) is a positive divine attribute, not a moral defect, because it is applied exclusively to God and identified as His name. | E072, E073, E074 | A word used 6 times and applied exclusively to God as a title and name cannot be a defect attributed to God; the text presents it as a defining characteristic of His covenant relationship. | Neutral | cmd-02 | cmd-03 |
| N014 | The Shema (Deu 6:4-5) is the positive counterpart to the first commandment: if YHWH is one, then no other god can exist alongside Him, and if He must be loved with all heart, soul, and might, then no capacity remains for another god. | E068, E076, E077 | A prohibition of other gods combined with a declaration that God is one and must receive total love leaves no room for divided allegiance. | Neutral | cmd-02 |  |
| N015 | Physical idols have no divine power because they are manufactured by humans from common materials and cannot see, hear, or act. | E101, E103, E104, E109, E110, E111 | Multiple texts state idols are the work of mens hands from wood, silver, and gold, and cannot see, hear, speak, or move. The only conclusion is that physical idols have no divine capability. | Neutral | cmd-02 | cmd-03 |
| N016 | The pagan entities called gods are not truly gods by nature, because both OT and NT texts deny their divine status while acknowledging they are called gods. | E081, E082, E101, E102, E117, E118, E126 | The OT calls them not God and elilim (worthless); Paul says they are called gods but by nature are no gods and an idol is nothing. Every text that addresses their nature denies it. | Neutral | cmd-02 |  |
| N017 | Behind idol worship stands demonic activity, because both OT and NT explicitly identify the recipients of pagan sacrifice as demons (shedim/daimonia). | E081, E105, E119 | Three independent texts in two testaments identify pagan sacrifice as sacrifice to demons. | Neutral | cmd-02 | cmd-03 |
| N018 | Jesus affirmed the continuing authority of the first commandment by quoting it as the first and great commandment and personally obeying it when tempted. | E112, E113, E114 | Jesus quoted the Shema as the first of all commandments and identified loving God totally as the first and great commandment, and He personally applied it by refusing to worship Satan. No alternative reading is possible. | Neutral | cmd-02 |  |
| N019 | The first commandment extends to the end of history, because Revelation frames the final conflict as a choice between worshipping the Creator and worshipping the beast. | E132, E133, E134, E138, E139 | Rev 14:7 calls all humanity to worship the Creator; Rev 14:9-10 warns against worshipping the beast; Rev 14:12 identifies the faithful as commandment-keepers. The eschatological structure presents the same choice as the first commandment. | Neutral | cmd-02 |  |
| N020 | Idolaters are excluded from the eschatological kingdom and face the second death. | E130, E136, E137 | Eph 5:5 states no idolater has inheritance in Gods kingdom; Rev 21:8 states idolaters face the second death; Rev 22:15 states idolaters are outside the city. Three texts in two authors state the same exclusion. | Neutral | cmd-02 | cmd-03 |
| N021 | The second commandment prohibits both the making of images (Exo 20:4) and the worship of images (Exo 20:5a). These are two distinct prohibitions within the same commandment. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N022 | The prohibition applies to likenesses of anything in the entire created order -- heaven, earth, and water cover all creation. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N023 | The theological basis for the prohibition is that God revealed Himself by voice, not by visible form, at Sinai. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N024 | God-commanded images (cherubim) were not objects of worship but markers of Gods presence and communication. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N025 | A God-commanded object that becomes an object of worship must be destroyed, as demonstrated by the bronze serpent. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N026 | The visiting of iniquity upon children is limited to those who continue hating God, because the text qualifies of them that hate me. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N027 | Gods mercy vastly exceeds His judgment: judgment extends to 3-4 generations while mercy extends to thousands (of generations). |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N028 | Individual judicial guilt is not transferable between generations: each person is accountable for their own sin. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N029 | The golden calf episode was a second commandment violation (worshipping God through an image) because Aaron declared the calf-worship a feast to the LORD. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N030 | Christ is the authorized image of the invisible God, not a human manufacture but a divine self-revelation. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N031 | The NT authors apply the second commandments prohibition to all peoples, not only to Israel. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-03 |  |
| N032 | The third commandments scope extends beyond verbal misuse to any form of misrepresenting God while bearing His name. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-04 |  |
| N033 | The consequence clause of the third commandment is grounded in Gods own character, not merely in legal statute. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-04 |  |
| N034 | The third commandment does not prohibit all use of Gods name in oaths but prohibits bearing it falsely or emptily. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-04 |  |
| N035 | Hallowed be thy name (Mat 6:9) is the positive counterpart to the third commandments prohibition. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-04 |  |
| N036 | Shav (H7723) linguistically connects the third and ninth commandments, linking the misuse of Gods name to bearing false testimony. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-04 | cmd-10 |
| N037 | The one who bears Gods name falsely will bear his own guilt -- the verb nasa (H5375) creates a wordplay linking the offense to its consequence. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-04 |  |
| N038 | The commandment itself provides a creation rationale (Exo 20:11), not a Sinai rationale, for the Sabbath. Creation events occurred before any nation existed. | Neutral | cmd-05 |  |

| N040 | Jesus's use of anthropos (G444, generic human being) in Mark 2:27 identifies the Sabbath's beneficiary as humanity generically, not a specific ethnic group. | E284 | Anthropos is the generic Greek word for human being. Jesus did not use Ioudaios or any ethnic term. The lexical meaning is a textual datum. | Neutral | cmd-05 |  |
| N041 | The pre-Sinai manna narrative (Exo 16:4-30) presents the Sabbath as operative before Sinai: God tests regarding His law, uses Sabbath vocabulary, rebukes violation, states the Sabbath was given -- all before Sinai. | E268,E269,E270,E271 | Exodus 16 precedes Exodus 19-20 chronologically. God's test/rebuke language presupposes existing standard. | Neutral | cmd-05 |  |
| N042 | Isaiah uses nekar (H5236, foreigner) rather than ger (H1616, resident alien) in Isa 56:3,6, extending the Sabbath invitation to persons more distant from Israel than those named in the commandment. | E261,E279,E280 | Nekar and ger are different Hebrew words with different semantic ranges. The lexical difference is verifiable. | Neutral | cmd-05 |  |
| N043 | The author of Hebrews switches from katapausis (G2663, 8 times in Heb 3-4) to sabbatismos (G4520, once in Heb 4:9) specifically in the verse declaring what remains for God's people. The -ismos suffix is standard Greek morphology denoting action/practice. | Neutral | cmd-05 |  |
| N044 | Exodus 20:8-11 and Deuteronomy 5:12-15 provide two different stated reasons for the same commandment: creation (Exo 20:11) and redemption from Egypt (Deu 5:15). | E262,E267 | Two passages cite two different events with the therefore (al-ken) construction. Every reader can see two different reasons. | Neutral | cmd-05 |  |
| N045 | The verb kabed (H3513) in the fifth commandment means to treat as heavy/significant/valuable, since the roots lexical meaning is to be heavy, the Piel stem is factitive (make heavy), and the LXX/NT consistently translate it as timao (G5091, to prize/fix a valuation upon). |  |  | Neutral | cmd-06 |  |
| N046 | The commandment applies equally to both parents, since every formulation names both father AND mother, penalty provisions specify father or mother, and Lev 19:3 reverses the order (mother first). |  |  | Neutral | cmd-06 |  |
| N047 | The same vocabulary used for honoring/fearing God is used for honoring/fearing parents -- kabed for both (Exo 20:12/Pro 3:9), yare for both (Lev 19:3/Deu 6:13) -- and Mal 1:6 explicitly argues from the former to the latter. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-06 |  |

| N049 | Jesus treated the fifth commandment as a current, binding divine command, calling it the commandment of God (Mat 15:3), the word of God (Mrk 7:13), and citing it to the rich young ruler (Mat 19:19; Mrk 10:19; Luk 18:20). |  |  | Neutral | cmd-06 |  |
| N050 | Paul treats the fifth commandment as binding on Gentile Christians, quoting it to the Ephesian and Colossian churches, identifying it as the first commandment with promise, and universalizing the land promise to the earth. |  |  | Neutral | cmd-06 |  |

| I002 | The attributes of the law (holy, just, good, spiritual, perfect, sure, true, eternal) mirror God's own attributes, indicating the Decalogue reflects God's character | I-A | E20-E27 list the law's attributes; Scripture attributes holiness, justice, goodness, and eternality to God | Combines law-attribute statements with God-attribute statements into a relational pattern | 5 | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| I003 | The Decalogue spans the entire Bible from Genesis (Gen 26:5) to Revelation (Rev 22:14), indicating universal and perpetual scope | I-A | E51 Abraham kept commandments pre-Sinai; E1-E8 Sinai; E22-E27 Psalms on permanence; E45-E46 prophetic heart-writing; E42-E44 Jesus/Paul continuity; E39-E41 Revelation | Synthesizes data points from across the entire canon into a single doctrinal statement | 5 | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
| I004 | Love is the animating principle of the Decalogue, with the first table expressing love for God and the second table expressing love for neighbor | I-A | E28 Jesus two love commands; E31 Paul quotes Decalogue as love's content; E32 all law fulfilled in love; E33 James royal law = love; E38 John love = keeping commandments | Mapping first table = love God and second table = love neighbor systematizes multiple E/N items with the two-tablet structure | 5 | Neutral | cmd-01 | cmd-12 |
| I005 | The new covenant fulfills the Decalogue by internalizing it through the Spirit rather than replacing it with different content | I-A | E45 my law on hearts; E46 Hebrews quotes same; E47 stone to heart; E48 Spirit enables statute-keeping; E50 righteousness of law fulfilled in Spirit-walkers | Synthesizes E45-E50 into a single theological framework of internalization rather than replacement | 5 | Neutral | cmd-01 | cmd-13, cmd-14 |
| I006 | The NT maintains a vocabulary distinction between entole (commandments including Decalogue) and dogma (ceremonial ordinances abolished), confirming the Decalogue's continuing authority | I-A | E39-E41 use entole for end-time commandment-keeping; Eph 2:15 and Col 2:14 use dogma for what was abolished | Systematizes lexical data into a doctrinal conclusion about continuing authority | 5 | Neutral | cmd-01 |  |
